name: Athletic Training
description: Introduces the profession of athletic training, covering injury prevention, evaluation, first aid, taping, rehabilitation, and career pathways. Students gain hands-on knowledge of caring for the physically active.

modules.title: Introduction to Athletic Training
modules.objectives: Define athletic training as a healthcare profession; describe the certified athletic trainer (ATC) role; identify diverse work settings.
modules.videoNote: Day in the Life of an Athletic Trainer
modules.assignment: Interview or shadow a certified athletic trainer. Write a one-page summary of their daily responsibilities, work setting, and career path.

modules.title: Anatomy for Athletic Trainers
modules.objectives: Identify major bones, joints, muscles, and ligaments relevant to sport injury; use correct anatomical terminology for location and movement.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Human Anatomy and Physiology
modules.assignment: Label a full musculoskeletal diagram identifying 20 key structures. Write a brief description of each structure's role in athletic movement.

modules.title: Injury Prevention Strategies
modules.objectives: Identify intrinsic and extrinsic risk factors for sport injury; explain how warm-up, conditioning, protective equipment, and rule enforcement reduce risk.
modules.assignment: Select one sport and create a comprehensive injury prevention protocol including pre-season conditioning, warm-up routines, and equipment guidelines.

modules.title: First Aid and Emergency Procedures
modules.objectives: Describe Emergency Action Plan (EAP) components; demonstrate first aid for common on-field emergencies; explain criteria for activating EMS.
modules.readingNote: American Red Cross — First Aid Basics
modules.videoNote: First Aid Basics for Sports Emergencies
modules.practiceNote: Red Cross — Online First Aid Simulation
modules.assignment: Draft a written Emergency Action Plan (EAP) for a high school athletic event. Include roles, communication steps, and emergency equipment locations.

modules.title: Taping and Bracing Techniques
modules.objectives: Explain purposes of prophylactic taping and bracing; describe ankle and wrist taping procedures; distinguish functional from prophylactic braces.
modules.videoNote: Ankle Taping Technique Step by Step
modules.assignment: Practice and document an ankle taping procedure on a partner using proper pre-wrap and tape layers. Write a self-critique of your technique.

modules.title: Rehabilitation Principles
modules.objectives: Describe phases of injury rehabilitation; explain PRICE protocol and progressive loading; identify evidence-based return-to-play criteria.
modules.assignment: Design a 4-week rehabilitation plan for a Grade II ankle sprain. Include phase goals, exercises, progression criteria, and return-to-play standards.

modules.title: Common Sports Injuries
modules.objectives: Identify mechanism, signs, symptoms, and initial management of the most common sport injuries by body region.
modules.assignment: Create a one-page reference card for 5 common sport injuries, including mechanism, signs/symptoms, field management, and referral criteria for each.

modules.title: Concussion Management
modules.objectives: Define concussion; identify signs and symptoms; explain CDC Heads Up protocols and the graduated return-to-play and return-to-learn protocol.
modules.readingNote: CDC Heads Up — Concussion Signs and Symptoms
modules.videoNote: Concussion: What You Need to Know
modules.practiceNote: CDC Heads Up — Coaches Concussion Training
modules.assignment: Complete the CDC Heads Up online concussion training. Write a policy recommendation for your school's concussion management protocol.

modules.title: Nutrition for Performance
modules.objectives: Explain macronutrient timing for athletic performance; describe competition hydration strategies; evaluate common sports supplements for safety and efficacy.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Energy in Our Food
modules.assignment: Analyze one week of an athlete's food diary. Calculate macronutrient ratios, assess pre/post-workout nutrition, and write personalized recommendations.

modules.title: Strength and Conditioning Basics
modules.objectives: Describe principles of strength and conditioning program design; explain periodization concepts; differentiate training cycle phases.
modules.assignment: Design a 12-week periodized strength and conditioning plan for a high school basketball player with macrocycle, mesocycle, and sample weekly schedule.

modules.title: Sports Pharmacology
modules.objectives: Identify classes of banned substances (PEDs); explain health risks of doping; describe WADA anti-doping policies and testing procedures.
modules.assignment: Research one class of banned substance. Create a fact sheet covering mechanism, health risks, detection methods, and consequences of a positive test.

modules.title: Legal and Ethical Issues in Athletic Training
modules.titleZh: 运动训练中的法律与伦理问题
modules.objectives: Explain scope of practice for ATs; describe negligence and informed consent; apply the NATA Code of Ethics to athletic training scenarios.
modules.practiceNote: NATA — Legal Resources for Athletic Trainers
modules.assignment: Analyze a hypothetical athletic training ethical dilemma. Apply the NATA Code of Ethics to propose and justify a course of action.

modules.title: Career Pathways in Athletic Training
modules.titleZh: 运动训练职业发展路径
modules.objectives: Describe educational requirements for ATC certification; identify diverse work settings; create a personal academic plan toward an athletic training career.
modules.assignment: Create a 4-year academic and extracurricular plan to qualify for an accredited athletic training master's program, including courses, volunteer hours, and certifications.

modules.title: Clinical Case Studies
modules.objectives: Apply course knowledge to evaluate, manage, and document athletic injuries using realistic clinical case scenarios.
modules.readingNote: NATA — Health Issues in Athletic Training
modules.assignment: Complete two written case study reports each including: injury evaluation (HOPS), working diagnosis, treatment plan, and referral decision.

quizQuestions.question: The professional credential for a certified athletic trainer is:
quizQuestions.explanation: ATC (Athletic Trainer Certified) is awarded by the Board of Certification to qualified athletic trainers.

quizQuestions.question: Athletic trainers primarily work with:
options: Physically active individuals across all ages and settings
quizQuestions.answer: Physically active individuals across all ages and settings
quizQuestions.explanation: Certified athletic trainers work in high schools, colleges, professional sports, military, clinics, and performing arts.

quizQuestions.question: Which organization sets national standards and advocates for the athletic training profession?
quizQuestions.explanation: The National Athletic Trainers' Association (NATA) is the professional membership organization for athletic trainers in the U.S.

quizQuestions.question: Athletic training is recognized as:
quizQuestions.explanation: Athletic training is an allied healthcare profession recognized by the AMA.

quizQuestions.question: Which service is within the scope of practice of a certified athletic trainer?
quizQuestions.explanation: ATs evaluate, manage, and rehabilitate sports injuries; diagnosis of systemic conditions and surgery are outside their scope.

quizQuestions.question: The anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) is located in the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The ACL is a major stabilizing ligament inside the knee joint, connecting femur to tibia.

quizQuestions.question: A sprain involves injury to:
quizQuestions.explanation: Sprains are ligament injuries (bone-to-bone), while strains involve muscles or tendons.

quizQuestions.question: The rotator cuff is a group of muscles and tendons surrounding the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The rotator cuff (supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, subscapularis) stabilizes the glenohumeral joint.

quizQuestions.question: A ball-and-socket joint allows movement:
quizQuestions.explanation: Ball-and-socket joints (hip, shoulder) permit movement in all planes.

quizQuestions.question: The Achilles tendon connects the calf muscles to the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The Achilles tendon attaches the gastrocnemius and soleus to the calcaneus (heel bone).

quizQuestions.question: Intrinsic risk factors for sport injury include:
options: Previous injury history and muscle imbalances
quizQuestions.answer: Previous injury history and muscle imbalances
quizQuestions.explanation: Intrinsic factors are internal athlete characteristics influencing injury susceptibility.

quizQuestions.question: Neuromuscular training programs like FIFA 11+ primarily prevent:
quizQuestions.explanation: FIFA 11+ and similar programs target landing mechanics and hip/core strength to reduce ACL injury rates.

quizQuestions.question: The most effective prevention strategy for overuse injuries is:
options: Gradually increasing training load with adequate recovery
quizQuestions.answer: Gradually increasing training load with adequate recovery
quizQuestions.explanation: Overuse injuries result from repetitive microtrauma exceeding tissue repair; progressive load management prevents them.

quizQuestions.question: Protective equipment (PPE) reduces injury by:
options: Absorbing and distributing forces away from vulnerable areas
quizQuestions.answer: Absorbing and distributing forces away from vulnerable areas
quizQuestions.explanation: PPE (helmets, pads, braces) dissipates impact forces to reduce injury risk.

quizQuestions.question: A pre-participation physical examination (PPE) is designed to:
options: Select the best athletes for competition
Screen athletes for conditions predisposing them to injury or sudden illness
quizQuestions.answer: Screen athletes for conditions predisposing them to injury or sudden illness
quizQuestions.explanation: The PPE identifies health conditions before athletic participation begins.

quizQuestions.question: The primary goal of an Emergency Action Plan (EAP) is to:
options: Ensure rapid, coordinated response to athletic emergencies
quizQuestions.answer: Ensure rapid, coordinated response to athletic emergencies
quizQuestions.explanation: An EAP outlines roles, communication, and equipment to enable a fast, organized emergency response.

quizQuestions.question: Signs of exertional heat stroke include:
options: Hot, dry skin and altered mental status
Low heart rate and normal temperature
quizQuestions.answer: Hot, dry skin and altered mental status
quizQuestions.explanation: Heat stroke is a life-threatening emergency: hyperthermia, cessation of sweating, and CNS dysfunction.

quizQuestions.question: Recommended first aid for a suspected spinal injury is:
options: Have the athlete walk off the field
Stabilize the head and neck; do not move the athlete
Apply ice and return to play
quizQuestions.answer: Stabilize the head and neck; do not move the athlete
quizQuestions.explanation: Suspected spinal injuries require immobilization — movement without proper precautions risks permanent paralysis.

quizQuestions.question: AED stands for:
quizQuestions.explanation: An AED delivers an electrical shock to restore normal heart rhythm during sudden cardiac arrest.

quizQuestions.question: When calling EMS (911) for an athletic emergency, you must provide:
options: Location, nature of emergency, athlete's condition, and your callback number
Team name and coach contact only
quizQuestions.answer: Location, nature of emergency, athlete's condition, and your callback number
quizQuestions.explanation: Effective 911 calls include precise location, emergency description, victim status, and caller contact.

quizQuestions.question: Prophylactic ankle taping primarily prevents:
options: Ankle sprains by limiting excessive inversion
quizQuestions.answer: Ankle sprains by limiting excessive inversion
quizQuestions.explanation: Prophylactic taping restricts excessive inversion motion, preventing lateral ankle sprains.

quizQuestions.question: Pre-wrap is applied before athletic tape to:
quizQuestions.explanation: Pre-wrap creates a protective barrier between the skin and rigid athletic tape.

quizQuestions.question: A functional brace (vs. prophylactic) is used:
options: To prevent injuries in healthy athletes
Post-injury to support a ligament-deficient joint during activity
quizQuestions.answer: Post-injury to support a ligament-deficient joint during activity
quizQuestions.explanation: Functional braces stabilize previously injured joints for safe return to activity.

quizQuestions.question: Heel-lock taping technique stabilizes the:
options: Subtalar joint to prevent inversion sprains
quizQuestions.answer: Subtalar joint to prevent inversion sprains
quizQuestions.explanation: Heel-lock strips anchor the heel and reinforce ankle taping to limit subtalar inversion.

quizQuestions.question: Ankle taping should be applied with the joint in:
quizQuestions.explanation: Taping in neutral/slight dorsiflexion prevents inversion without restricting normal motion.

quizQuestions.question: The acute inflammatory phase of injury healing lasts approximately:
quizQuestions.explanation: Acute inflammation begins immediately after injury and typically resolves within 72 hours.

quizQuestions.question: During the proliferative phase of healing, the primary tissue deposited is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Fibroblasts lay down type III collagen forming immature scar tissue during the proliferative phase (days 3–21).

quizQuestions.question: Progressive loading in rehabilitation means:
options: Systematically increasing exercise demand as tissue heals
Avoiding all exercise until fully healed
quizQuestions.answer: Systematically increasing exercise demand as tissue heals
quizQuestions.explanation: Progressive loading stimulates tissue remodeling without exceeding the healing tissue's tolerance.

quizQuestions.question: A functional return-to-play criterion requires the athlete to:
options: Demonstrate full strength, ROM, and sport-specific function
quizQuestions.answer: Demonstrate full strength, ROM, and sport-specific function
quizQuestions.explanation: Safe return requires objective criteria: full range of motion, symmetrical strength, and sport-specific testing.

quizQuestions.question: PRICE differs from RICE by adding:
quizQuestions.explanation: PRICE = Protection, Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation. Protection prevents further injury.

quizQuestions.question: A Grade II ankle sprain involves:
options: Partial ligament tear with moderate pain and swelling
Complete ligament rupture with severe instability
quizQuestions.answer: Partial ligament tear with moderate pain and swelling
quizQuestions.explanation: Grade II sprains involve partial ligament tearing with moderate pain, swelling, and some instability.

quizQuestions.question: A concussion is classified as:
quizQuestions.explanation: A concussion is a mild TBI caused by biomechanical forces that temporarily alter brain function.

quizQuestions.question: Patellofemoral pain syndrome is most associated with:
options: Overuse, muscle imbalances, and abnormal patellar tracking
quizQuestions.answer: Overuse, muscle imbalances, and abnormal patellar tracking
quizQuestions.explanation: Patellofemoral pain results from abnormal loading of the patellofemoral joint, often from training errors and biomechanical factors.

quizQuestions.question: Iliotibial (IT) band syndrome most commonly occurs in:
quizQuestions.explanation: IT band syndrome results from repetitive knee flexion-extension causing friction over the lateral femoral condyle.

quizQuestions.question: Stress fractures are overuse injuries most commonly affecting:
quizQuestions.explanation: Repetitive stress exceeding bone remodeling capacity causes stress fractures, most often in weight-bearing bones.

quizQuestions.question: Concussion is diagnosed primarily by:
options: Clinical evaluation of signs and symptoms
quizQuestions.answer: Clinical evaluation of signs and symptoms
quizQuestions.explanation: Standard neuroimaging appears normal in most concussions; diagnosis is clinical.

quizQuestions.question: CDC's 'When in Doubt, Sit Them Out' means:
options: Rest only if the athlete loses consciousness
Remove any suspected concussion athlete from play immediately
Athletes may return after 15 minutes if symptoms resolve
quizQuestions.answer: Remove any suspected concussion athlete from play immediately
quizQuestions.explanation: The CDC recommends immediate removal to prevent second-impact syndrome.

quizQuestions.question: Second-impact syndrome occurs when:
options: An athlete sustains two concussions years apart
A second head injury occurs before full recovery from the first concussion
An athlete is concussed twice in the same game with no symptoms
quizQuestions.answer: A second head injury occurs before full recovery from the first concussion
quizQuestions.explanation: Second-impact syndrome can cause rapid, catastrophic brain swelling and is potentially fatal.

quizQuestions.question: Common concussion symptoms include all EXCEPT:
quizQuestions.explanation: Concussion symptoms include headache, confusion, fatigue, and sensitivity — not improved energy or focus.

quizQuestions.question: Graduated return-to-play after concussion requires:
options: Return to full contact when symptoms resolve the same day
Each step at least 24 hours apart with regression if symptoms return
A fixed 2-week wait regardless of symptoms
quizQuestions.answer: Each step at least 24 hours apart with regression if symptoms return
quizQuestions.explanation: The graduated protocol ensures stepwise return; any symptom recurrence requires stepping back.

quizQuestions.question: Carbohydrate loading before endurance events aims to:
quizQuestions.explanation: Carbohydrate loading saturates glycogen stores, delaying fatigue during prolonged endurance events.

quizQuestions.question: Electrolytes lost most heavily in sweat are:
quizQuestions.explanation: Sweat contains primarily sodium and chloride, which must be replenished during prolonged exercise.

quizQuestions.question: Optimal protein intake for strength-trained athletes is approximately:
quizQuestions.explanation: Research supports 1.6–2.2 g/kg/day for maximizing muscle protein synthesis in resistance athletes.

quizQuestions.question: Post-exercise nutrition within 30–60 minutes should prioritize:
options: Carbohydrates and protein to replenish glycogen and stimulate repair
quizQuestions.answer: Carbohydrates and protein to replenish glycogen and stimulate repair
quizQuestions.explanation: Post-exercise carbohydrates restore glycogen; protein provides amino acids for muscle repair.

quizQuestions.question: Creatine monohydrate has strongest evidence for improving:
quizQuestions.explanation: Creatine enhances phosphocreatine resynthesis, improving brief maximal efforts like sprints and heavy lifting.

quizQuestions.question: Periodization divides training into cycles called:
quizQuestions.explanation: Periodization organizes training into nested cycles: macrocycle (season), mesocycle (blocks), microcycle (weekly).

quizQuestions.question: The off-season phase in periodization focuses on:
quizQuestions.explanation: The off-season emphasizes general preparation: aerobic base, correcting imbalances, and recovering from competition.

quizQuestions.question: A deload week involves:
options: Reducing volume and intensity for recovery and supercompensation
quizQuestions.answer: Reducing volume and intensity for recovery and supercompensation
quizQuestions.explanation: Deload weeks reduce fatigue and allow supercompensation, resulting in improved performance after the recovery period.

quizQuestions.question: The SAID principle (Specific Adaptation to Imposed Demands) means:
options: The body adapts specifically to the demands placed upon it
Rest produces the same adaptations as training
quizQuestions.answer: The body adapts specifically to the demands placed upon it
quizQuestions.explanation: SAID is the basis of specificity — training must match the demands of the target sport.

quizQuestions.question: HIIT improves performance by:
options: Alternating maximal effort with rest to improve both aerobic and anaerobic capacity
quizQuestions.answer: Alternating maximal effort with rest to improve both aerobic and anaerobic capacity
quizQuestions.explanation: HIIT's high-intensity intervals and recovery periods develop multiple energy systems.

quizQuestions.question: WADA stands for:
quizQuestions.explanation: WADA is the international body responsible for promoting and monitoring anti-doping globally.

quizQuestions.question: Anabolic-androgenic steroids improve muscle mass by:
quizQuestions.explanation: Anabolic steroids mimic testosterone, accelerating protein synthesis but causing serious health risks.

quizQuestions.question: Blood doping involves:
options: Artificially increasing red blood cells to enhance endurance
quizQuestions.answer: Artificially increasing red blood cells to enhance endurance
quizQuestions.explanation: Blood doping increases oxygen-carrying capacity and is banned by WADA.

quizQuestions.question: Which supplement is legal and widely used to reduce perceived exertion?
quizQuestions.explanation: Caffeine is a legal ergogenic aid that reduces perceived exertion and enhances endurance.

quizQuestions.question: WADA's 3 criteria for banning a substance include performance enhancement, health risk, and:
options: Violation of the spirit of sport
quizQuestions.answer: Violation of the spirit of sport
quizQuestions.explanation: A substance must meet at least 2 of WADA's 3 criteria to be prohibited.

quizQuestions.question: Negligence in athletic training is:
options: Failure to provide the standard of care expected of a reasonable AT
quizQuestions.answer: Failure to provide the standard of care expected of a reasonable AT
quizQuestions.explanation: Negligence requires: duty, breach of duty, causation, and harm.

quizQuestions.question: Informed consent requires that athletes:
options: Automatically consent by joining a team
Understand and agree to treatment after learning risks and alternatives
quizQuestions.answer: Understand and agree to treatment after learning risks and alternatives
quizQuestions.explanation: Informed consent means patients receive clear information and freely agree before treatment begins.

quizQuestions.question: HIPAA in athletic training protects:
options: An AT's right to refuse treatment
quizQuestions.explanation: HIPAA mandates protection of patients' private health information.

quizQuestions.question: Which situation represents a potential NATA ethical violation?
options: Referring an athlete to a physician
Pressuring an athlete to return before medically cleared
quizQuestions.answer: Pressuring an athlete to return before medically cleared
quizQuestions.explanation: ATs must prioritize athlete health; pressuring early return violates patient welfare and NATA ethics.

quizQuestions.question: Scope of practice for athletic trainers is defined by:
options: State practice acts and NATA position statements
quizQuestions.answer: State practice acts and NATA position statements
quizQuestions.explanation: Each state's practice act defines legal AT practice; NATA provides professional standards.

quizQuestions.question: To become a certified athletic trainer (ATC), candidates must:
options: Graduate from a CAATE-accredited master's program and pass the BOC exam
quizQuestions.answer: Graduate from a CAATE-accredited master's program and pass the BOC exam
quizQuestions.explanation: Since 2022, entry-level AT practice requires a CAATE-accredited master's degree and BOC exam passage.

quizQuestions.question: The largest work setting for athletic trainers is:
quizQuestions.explanation: The majority of certified ATs work in secondary and collegiate settings.

quizQuestions.question: Prerequisite coursework for AT master's programs typically includes:
quizQuestions.explanation: AT programs require a science foundation in anatomy, physiology, biology, and kinesiology.

quizQuestions.question: CAATE stands for:
options: Council for Athletic and Academic Training Excellence
Commission on Accreditation of Athletic Training Education
Certified Alliance of Athletic Training Educators
quizQuestions.answer: Commission on Accreditation of Athletic Training Education
quizQuestions.explanation: CAATE accredits athletic training programs to ensure competency standards are met.

quizQuestions.question: Continuing education units (CEUs) are required to:
options: Maintain BOC certification and stay current in the field
quizQuestions.answer: Maintain BOC certification and stay current in the field
quizQuestions.explanation: BOC-certified ATs must complete 50 CEUs every 2 years to maintain their credential.

quizQuestions.question: HOPS in athletic injury evaluation stands for:
quizQuestions.explanation: HOPS is a systematic injury evaluation framework.

quizQuestions.question: The Ottawa Ankle Rules determine when:
options: X-rays are indicated for potential ankle fractures
Athletes can return to play after sprains
quizQuestions.answer: X-rays are indicated for potential ankle fractures
quizQuestions.explanation: Ottawa Ankle Rules are evidence-based clinical guidelines for determining radiograph necessity.

quizQuestions.question: The anterior drawer test of the ankle assesses the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The anterior drawer test stresses the ATFL, the most commonly injured lateral ankle ligament.

quizQuestions.question: A positive Lachman test suggests injury to the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The Lachman test is the most sensitive clinical test for ACL injury.

quizQuestions.question: SOAP notes stand for:
quizQuestions.explanation: SOAP notes are a standard medical documentation format used by healthcare providers.

questions.question: The professional credential for a certified athletic trainer is:
questions.explanation: ATC is awarded by the Board of Certification to qualified athletic trainers.

questions.question: A sprain involves injury to:
questions.explanation: Sprains are ligament injuries; strains involve muscles or tendons.

questions.question: The ACL is located in the:
questions.explanation: The anterior cruciate ligament is a major stabilizing ligament inside the knee joint.

questions.question: Which best prevents overuse injuries?
options: Gradually increasing load with adequate recovery
questions.answer: Gradually increasing load with adequate recovery
questions.explanation: Overuse injuries result from exceeding tissue repair capacity; progressive load management prevents them.

questions.question: AED stands for:
questions.explanation: An AED delivers electrical shock to restore normal heart rhythm during cardiac arrest.

questions.question: Pre-wrap is used before athletic tape to:
questions.explanation: Pre-wrap creates a protective barrier between skin and rigid tape.

questions.question: The acute inflammatory phase of healing lasts approximately:
questions.explanation: Acute inflammation begins immediately after injury and resolves within approximately 72 hours.

questions.question: DOMS peaks approximately:
questions.explanation: Delayed Onset Muscle Soreness peaks 24–72 hours after intense or novel exercise.

questions.question: Concussion is most accurately diagnosed by:
options: Clinical evaluation of signs and symptoms
questions.answer: Clinical evaluation of signs and symptoms
questions.explanation: Standard imaging appears normal in most concussions; diagnosis is clinical.

questions.question: Carbohydrate loading before endurance events aims to:
questions.explanation: Carbohydrate loading saturates glycogen stores to delay fatigue during endurance events.

questions.question: Periodization divides training into:
questions.explanation: Periodization uses nested training cycles to manage volume and intensity across a season.

questions.question: WADA is responsible for:
questions.explanation: WADA coordinates anti-doping efforts globally and maintains the Prohibited List.

questions.question: Informed consent in sports medicine means:
options: Athletes consent automatically by joining a team
Athletes agree to treatment after understanding risks and alternatives
Emergency treatment always requires prior consent
questions.answer: Athletes agree to treatment after understanding risks and alternatives
questions.explanation: Informed consent requires clear communication of treatment options, risks, and benefits before agreement.

questions.question: SOAP notes stand for:
questions.explanation: SOAP is a standard healthcare documentation format.

questions.question: The Achilles tendon connects to the:
questions.explanation: The Achilles tendon attaches the gastrocnemius and soleus to the heel bone.

questions.question: HOPS stands for:
questions.explanation: HOPS is a systematic injury evaluation framework used by athletic trainers.

questions.question: A positive Lachman test indicates injury to the:
questions.explanation: The Lachman test is the most sensitive clinical test for ACL rupture.

questions.question: Second-impact syndrome occurs when:
options: A second head injury occurs before full recovery from the first
An athlete is hit twice with no symptoms
questions.answer: A second head injury occurs before full recovery from the first
questions.explanation: Second-impact syndrome can cause rapid, catastrophic brain swelling and is potentially fatal.

questions.question: The rotator cuff surrounds the:
questions.explanation: The four rotator cuff muscles stabilize the glenohumeral (shoulder) joint.

questions.question: PRICE adds which step to RICE?
questions.explanation: PRICE = Protection, Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation. Protection prevents further injury.

questions.question: Entry-level ATs must graduate from:
questions.explanation: Since 2022, entry-level AT practice requires a CAATE-accredited master's degree plus BOC exam.

questions.question: A Grade II ankle sprain involves:
options: Partial tear with moderate pain and swelling
questions.answer: Partial tear with moderate pain and swelling
questions.explanation: Grade II sprains involve partial tearing with moderate pain, swelling, and some instability.

questions.question: The anterior drawer test assesses the:
questions.explanation: The anterior drawer test stresses the ATFL, the most commonly injured lateral ankle ligament.

questions.question: Blood doping involves:
options: Artificially increasing red blood cells to boost endurance
questions.answer: Artificially increasing red blood cells to boost endurance
questions.explanation: Blood doping increases oxygen-carrying capacity and is banned by WADA.

questions.question: Graduated return to play after concussion requires:
options: Full contact return when symptoms resolve that day
Each step at least 24 hours apart, regression if symptoms return
questions.answer: Each step at least 24 hours apart, regression if symptoms return
questions.explanation: The graduated protocol ensures safe stepwise return to play.

questions.question: Stress fractures most commonly occur in:
questions.explanation: Repetitive stress exceeding bone remodeling capacity causes stress fractures in weight-bearing bones.

questions.question: HIPAA protects:
options: An AT's right to refuse treatment
questions.explanation: HIPAA mandates protection of patients' private health information.

questions.question: Prophylactic ankle taping primarily prevents:
questions.explanation: Prophylactic taping limits inversion to protect against lateral ankle sprains.

questions.question: A deload week is designed to:
options: Reduce volume and intensity for recovery and supercompensation
questions.answer: Reduce volume and intensity for recovery and supercompensation
questions.explanation: Deload weeks reduce accumulated fatigue, allowing the body to supercompensate beyond previous levels.

questions.question: An Emergency Action Plan ensures:
options: Rapid, coordinated response to life-threatening emergencies
questions.answer: Rapid, coordinated response to life-threatening emergencies
questions.explanation: EAPs define roles, communication protocols, and equipment locations for emergencies.

questions.question: Optimal post-exercise nutrition window is within:
questions.explanation: The 30–60 minute post-exercise window maximizes glycogen resynthesis and muscle protein synthesis.

questions.question: The SAID principle states the body:
options: Adapts specifically to the demands placed on it
questions.answer: Adapts specifically to the demands placed on it
questions.explanation: SAID = Specific Adaptation to Imposed Demands — training must match desired outcomes.

questions.question: Proving negligence in athletic training does NOT require:
options: Duty of care owed to the athlete
Causal link between breach and harm
questions.explanation: Negligence is proven by duty, breach, causation, and damages — intent is irrelevant.

questions.question: IT band syndrome most commonly affects:
questions.explanation: Repetitive knee flexion-extension friction of the IT band over the lateral femoral condyle causes IT band syndrome.

questions.question: Creatine monohydrate is most effective for:
questions.explanation: Creatine enhances phosphocreatine resynthesis, improving brief maximal efforts.
